claims.claim_text: The opening anecdote describes an unattributed 2:00 AM payments-company production upgrade failure on the third control-plane node.
claims.span_hint: line 21 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: At 2:00 AM, a platform engineer at a payments company watched the incident channel fill with messages that all pointed back to one mistake: cluster upgrades were still being handled like individual maintenance projects. One engineer had upgraded a staging cluster successfully, copied most of the same commands into production, and hit an etcd membership problem on the third control-plane node. The failure was not only a command typo. The real failure was that every cluster had become a slightly different snowflake, with upgrade notes in tickets, bootstrap flags in old runbooks, and recovery knowledge stored in whoever happened to be on call.
claims.suggested_rewrite: During cluster upgrades, platform teams often discover that the technical failure is only a symptom of a deeper lifecycle problem: every cluster has drifted into a slightly different snowflake, with upgrade notes in tickets, bootstrap flags in old runbooks, and recovery knowledge concentrated in whoever happens to be on call.
claims.rationale: This is a composite-style war story with a precise time, company type, production context, and control-plane-node detail but no attributable incident source.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API is a Kubernetes/SIG Cluster Lifecycle subproject providing declarative APIs and tooling for provisioning, upgrading, and operating multiple Kubernetes clusters.
claims.span_hint: lines 23 and 499 | sections: Why This Module Matters; Did You Know
claims.rationale: The upstream README directly states Cluster API's SIG origin, Kubernetes-style API model, and lifecycle-management scope.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API distinguishes a management cluster that runs CAPI/providers from workload clusters whose lifecycle is managed by CAPI resources.
claims.span_hint: line 33 | section: The Control-Loop Model for Clusters
claims.rationale: The Cluster API concepts page defines management clusters and workload clusters using these responsibilities.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API recommends separating the management cluster from application workloads and using production backup and disaster recovery policies.
claims.span_hint: lines 35, 69, 237, 450, 512 | sections: Control-Loop Model; Hands-On; Production Operations; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The quick-start documentation recommends keeping the management cluster separated from application workloads and using backup/DR for production use.

claims.claim_text: A CAPI-created workload cluster can have machines and a control plane before node readiness is complete because a CNI must still be installed.
claims.span_hint: lines 75, 170, 185, 282, 293, 510, 524, 591 | sections: Control-Loop Model; Worked Example; Walkthrough; Common Mistakes; Quiz; Exercise
claims.rationale: The quick-start explicitly warns that the control plane will not be ready until a CNI is installed and includes CNI deployment steps.

claims.claim_text: ClusterResourceSet can automatically apply user-defined resources such as CNI or CSI resources to matching workload clusters.
claims.span_hint: lines 75 and 185 | sections: Control-Loop Model; Worked Example
claims.rationale: The ClusterResourceSet documentation says it can automatically apply resources such as CNI/CSI to matching clusters.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API splits lifecycle responsibilities across core, infrastructure, bootstrap, and control-plane providers.
claims.span_hint: lines 90-92 and 122-127 | section: Providers and Resource Anatomy
claims.rationale: The concepts page defines infrastructure, bootstrap, and control-plane providers and describes how Cluster API can be extended by provider types.

claims.claim_text: Cluster, Machine, MachineDeployment, MachineSet, and MachineHealthCheck have the resource relationships and lifecycle roles described in the module.
claims.span_hint: lines 116-120, 118, 120, 154, 187, 202, 322, 452 | sections: Providers and Resource Anatomy; Worked Example; Walkthrough; Production Operations
claims.rationale: The Cluster API concepts page defines these resources and describes MachineDeployment, MachineSet, Machine, and MachineHealthCheck behavior.

claims.claim_text: The kubeadm-based stack uses KubeadmControlPlane and kubeadm bootstrap, while other bootstrap/control-plane providers such as Talos, k0s, and MicroK8s exist.
claims.span_hint: line 129 | section: Providers and Resource Anatomy
claims.rationale: The provider list includes kubeadm plus alternative bootstrap and control-plane providers including k0s, MicroK8s, and Talos.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API has infrastructure providers for AWS, Azure, GCP, vSphere, Metal3, Docker, OpenStack, Hetzner, and many other platforms.
claims.span_hint: lines 131-142 | section: Providers and Resource Anatomy
claims.rationale: The upstream provider implementation list names the infrastructure providers covered by the table.

claims.claim_text: CAPD is a Docker-based reference/development provider and is not designed for production use.
claims.span_hint: lines 131, 140, 218, 500, 509 | sections: Providers and Resource Anatomy; Hands-On; Did You Know; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The CAPD README directly states that the Docker provider is a reference implementation and not designed for production.

claims.claim_text: clusterctl init installs provider components and clusterctl generate cluster returns YAML for creating a workload cluster.
claims.span_hint: lines 237, 251, 267 | section: Hands-On Walkthrough with CAPD
claims.rationale: The quick-start documents clusterctl init provider installation and clusterctl generate cluster output.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API upgrades commonly use rolling replacement: update control-plane version first, then worker MachineDeployments, with machine images and provider support checked.
claims.span_hint: lines 324 and 478 | sections: Hands-On Walkthrough; Production Operations
claims.rationale: The CAPI upgrade guide describes control-plane then worker upgrades, rolling updates, immutable MachineTemplates, and matching machine images.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes upgrades must respect version-skew policy.
claims.span_hint: lines 324 and 478 | sections: Hands-On Walkthrough; Production Operations
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes version-skew policy is the authoritative source for supported component skew during upgrades.

claims.claim_text: Cluster API machine deletion drains nodes, deletes infrastructure and bootstrap objects, and deletes the workload-cluster Node object.
claims.span_hint: lines 341, 352, 487, 572 | sections: Hands-On Walkthrough; Production Operations; Quiz
claims.rationale: The machine deletion documentation describes drain, infrastructure object deletion, bootstrap object deletion, and Node deletion.

claims.claim_text: ClusterClass defines reusable cluster topology with templates and variables for version, control-plane replicas, workers, and patches.
claims.span_hint: lines 356-379, 501, 515, 532 | sections: ClusterClass, GitOps, and Fleet Design; Did You Know; Common Mistakes; Quiz
claims.rationale: The ClusterClass authoring guide describes templates, topology variables, worker classes, patches, and generated underlying resources.

claims.claim_text: Terraform codifies infrastructure APIs into declarative configuration files and uses execution plans, resource graphs, and state.
claims.span_hint: lines 381-391, 393, 556 | section: ClusterClass, GitOps, and Fleet Design
claims.rationale: The Terraform README describes declarative configuration, execution plans, resource graphs, and state-related workflow.

claims.claim_text: Crossplane is a cloud-native control-plane framework that can orchestrate applications and infrastructure through a declarative API.
claims.span_hint: lines 381-393 and 556 | section: ClusterClass, GitOps, and Fleet Design
claims.rationale: The Crossplane README describes it as a framework for building cloud-native control planes with a configurable declarative API.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD automated sync does not prune by default, but automatic pruning can delete resources no longer defined in Git when enabled.
claims.span_hint: lines 395 and 418 | section: ClusterClass, GitOps, and Fleet Design
claims.rationale: Argo CD's automated sync documentation directly describes automatic pruning behavior and the prune field.

claims.claim_text: clusterctl move can move Cluster API workload-cluster objects between management clusters, but it is not a backup/restore replacement.
claims.span_hint: lines 420, 502, 548 | sections: ClusterClass, GitOps, and Fleet Design; Did You Know; Quiz
claims.rationale: The clusterctl move documentation says it moves CAPI workload-cluster objects and warns that it is not designed as a backup/restore solution.

claims.claim_text: MachineHealthCheck can identify unhealthy machines and trigger remediation, with maxUnhealthy limiting remediation during broader unhealthy states.
claims.span_hint: lines 452, 476, 514, 564 | sections: Production Operations; Common Mistakes; Quiz
claims.rationale: The MachineHealthCheck documentation describes unhealthy-condition timeouts, remediation, and maxUnhealthy short-circuiting.

claims.claim_text: etcd is Kubernetes' backing store for cluster data, and Kubernetes recommends having an etcd backup plan.
claims.span_hint: lines 69, 420, 450, 512, 548 | sections: Control-Loop Model; Fleet Design; Production Operations; Common Mistakes; Quiz
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes etcd operations guide states that etcd is the backing store for cluster data and calls for backup planning.

claims.claim_text: CAPI concepts include external or managed control planes offered by systems such as GKE, AKS, EKS, or IKS.
claims.span_hint: line 491 | section: Production Operations and Senior-Level Trade-Offs
claims.rationale: The Cluster API concepts page explicitly lists GKE, AKS, EKS, and IKS as examples of external or managed control planes.

claims.claim_text: Rancher has a documented Cluster API integration path via Rancher Turtles.
claims.span_hint: line 493 | section: Production Operations and Senior-Level Trade-Offs
claims.rationale: Rancher's documentation directly describes Rancher Turtles as an integration between Cluster API and Rancher.

claims.claim_text: Gardener has a machine-controller-manager listed as a Cluster API or Machine API adopter in the upstream Cluster API provider list.
claims.span_hint: line 493 | section: Production Operations and Senior-Level Trade-Offs
claims.rationale: The Cluster API provider list includes Gardener Machine Controller Manager under API adopters.

further_reading.title: Cluster API upstream project
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for the project scope, SIG ownership, and declarative cluster lifecycle model.

further_reading.title: Cluster API concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines management clusters, workload clusters, providers, Machines, MachineDeployments, MachineSets, and MachineHealthChecks.

further_reading.title: Cluster API quick start
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers clusterctl initialization, Docker provider lab flow, workload cluster creation, kubeconfig retrieval, and CNI installation.

further_reading.title: Writing a ClusterClass
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains reusable topology, templates, variables, worker classes, patches, and ClusterClass-generated resources.

further_reading.title: Upgrading management and workload clusters
further_reading.why_relevant: Supports the module's upgrade guidance around version support, machine images, control-plane upgrades, and worker rollouts.

notes: No existing section citation pool was present for platform-toolkits-infrastructure-networking-platforms, so proposed URLs are included directly. Quiz prompts, exercise steps, diagrams, and code blocks were not seeded as standalone claims except where the same factual claim appears in prose or tables.
